
# Methods
## Mark-recapture sampling

Harrison Lake and FMCC jointly released a total of 3,494 mussels of four species of freshwater mussels on October 11, 2023 in the Shenandoah River at the Bentonville site (38.84376127456249, -78.3387504059278). 
Of these releases, 265 were uniquely tagged such that the individual mussel could be later identified. 
In 2024, the site was sampled four times from June to October. 
*description of sampling here - see notes from Jess*. 
For each mussel encountered, we measured its overall length and recorded the unique tag number. 
Untagged mussels were not tagged. 
Were returned to stream, etc.

## POPAN description
We were interested primarily in estimating survival of the release cohort over time and the corresponding abundance on each sampling occasion.
Consequently, we used the POPAN mark-recapture framework, which allows for the estimation of both apparent surival and population size, to analyze our mark-recapture data.
The POPAN model is a parameterization of the Jolly-Seber model *reference*. 
It models Jolly-Seber in terms of a super population (N) and probability of entry (see link below for Gary White overview and Schwarz and Arnason (1996) for original paper).
It has four model parameters:

1) Super population 
2) probability of entry into the super population 
3) apparent survival
4) capture probability

During field sampling, we only recorded already tagged individuals without tagging new mussels (specify that this because there really weren't new mussels yet?)
Consequently, we considered the super population to be the initial release cohort.
We also assumed that pent was 0 because no new animals can enter the initial cohort. 
Thus, neither of these two model parameters were estimated during the anlaysis. 

## Data Analysis
Data for uniquely tagged individuals released in 2023 were combined with data from the four sampling occasions in 2024. 
A set of canddiate models were developed under the POPAN framework for estimating abundance and apparent surival of the 2023 release cohort.
Each candidate model represents a set of hypotheses regarding the four model parameters of the POPAN model (e.g., survival varies over time or is constant).
For all models, the probability of entry into the super population was fixed to 0.
We constructed candidate models where:

1) super population was either constant or varied between facilities,
2) apparent survival was either constant, varied over time, varied by facility, or varied over time and by facility,
3) capture probability was either constant, varied over time, or varied by facility.
This resulted in X total models in the canddiate model set for each species.
To avoid introducing species as a factor in our models, we analyzed each of the four species released in 2023 separately rather than as a combined data set. 

We analyzed our set of candidate models using Program MARK (White and Burnham 1999) to determine which model had the highest likelihood using an information criteria approach that balances the goodness-of-fit with model complexity to rank the models. *SEE BURNHAM BOOKS for language*.

or

We used an information criteria approach to determine the model with the highest likelihood (i.e., balance of goodness of fit and model complexity) (Villella, Smith, and Lemarié 2004; Meador, Peterson, and Wisniewski 2011) *confirm these references*

We used RMARK (an R package runs MARK) to build and analyze models.
The likelihood of each candidate model was calculated using Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC) (Akaike 1973) modified for small sample sizes (AICc) (Sugiura 1978): 
$$𝐴𝐼𝐶𝑐=−2log(𝐿(θ̂))+2𝐾(𝐾+1)𝑛−𝐾−1$$

where 𝐿(θ̂) is the likelihood of the parameter estimates, given the data, K is the number of parameters, and n is the sample size. 
Normally, the best model is the one with the lowest AICc score and other models are considered competing if ΔAIC <2.0. **reference here** 
However, top models for both species had issues estimating survival, especially on the last occasion. 
This is not uncommon in parameters that are modeled as fully time-dependent, especially when capture rates are relativley low. *reference?*
For purposes of estiamting survial/abundance, we decided to use the subset of the top model where time was remvoed as a factor for apparent survival (i.e, survival does not vary with time).
We based all estimates of survival and abundance on this model for each species.

Finally, many of the mussels released by FMCC in 2023 were either untagged or tagged in a manner that did not allow for the specific mussel to be identified in the future (e.g., green squares with no unique identifier).
We assumed that the survival estimates for uniquely tagged mussels was the same for non-unique and untagged mussels released by FMCC.
Thus, we applied the estimated survival rates to the entire release cohort for FMCC (unique, non-unique, and untagged) to estimate abundance for all mussels for each species.
